per_user_turn.result_preview:
Done. Here's what was added:

**`packages/common/interceptors/response-time.interceptor.ts`** — The new interceptor:
- Records `Date.now()` before the handler runs
- Uses `tap` (rxjs) to set the `X-Response-Time: <elapsed>ms` header after the response is produced
- Only acts in `'http'` context (skips WebSocket/RPC contexts)
- Handles both Express (`setHeader`) and Fastify (`header`) response APIs via duck typing

per_user_turn.result_preview:
Usage:

```typescript
// Always add the header (existing behaviour)
new ResponseTimeInterceptor()

// Only add header when response takes longer than 200ms
new ResponseTimeInterceptor({ threshold: 200 })
```

per_user_turn.result_preview:
Created `sample/01-cats-app/src/core/interceptors/response-time.interceptor.ts`:

- Records `Date.now()` before the handler runs
- Uses `tap` (which runs after the handler completes) to set the `X-Response-Time` header with the elapsed milliseconds
- Gets the HTTP response via `context.switchToHttp().getResponse()`

Registered it globally in `core.module.ts` via `APP_INTERCEPTOR`, so it applies to all routes automatically.

per_user_turn.result_preview:
The interceptor now accepts an optional `ResponseTimeOptions` object. Usage examples:

```typescript
// Default header name (X-Response-Time)
new ResponseTimeInterceptor()

// Custom header name
new ResponseTimeInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Request-Duration' })
```

To use a custom name in the module:

```typescript
{ provide: APP_INTERCEPTOR, useFactory: () => new ResponseTimeInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Request-Duration' }) }
```

per_user_turn.result_preview:
`threshold` defaults to `0` (always adds the header, preserving existing behavior). To only add the header for slow requests:

```typescript
// Only set header when response takes longer than 200ms
new ResponseTimeInterceptor({ threshold: 200 })
```

per_user_turn.result_preview:
Done. Here's what was added:

**`packages/common/interceptors/response-time.interceptor.ts`** — the new `ResponseTimeInterceptor` class. It:
1. Records `Date.now()` before the handler runs
2. Uses `tap` (after the observable emits) to set `X-Response-Time: <N>ms` on the response
3. Guards with `!response.headersSent` to avoid throwing on already-flushed responses (e.g. streaming)

**`packages/common/interceptors/index.ts`** — barrel re-export.

**`packages/common/index.ts`** — added `export * fr

per_user_turn.result_preview:
Done. Here's what was added:

**`sample/01-cats-app/src/core/interceptors/timing.interceptor.ts`** — new `TimingInterceptor` that:
1. Records `Date.now()` before the handler runs
2. Uses `tap` to set `X-Response-Time: <N>ms` on the response after the handler completes

**`sample/01-cats-app/src/main.ts`** — registered `TimingInterceptor` globally via `app.useGlobalInterceptors(new TimingInterceptor())`, so it applies to every route without needing `@UseInterceptors` on individual controllers.

per_user_turn.result_preview:
The interceptor now accepts an optional `TimingInterceptorOptions` object. Usage examples:

```ts
// Default header name: X-Response-Time
new TimingInterceptor()

// Custom header name
new TimingInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Request-Duration' })
```

The `main.ts` registration doesn't need to change since the options object is optional with a sensible default.

per_user_turn.result_preview:
`thresholdMs` defaults to `0`, so existing behavior is preserved — the header is always added unless a threshold is specified. Example:

```ts
// Only add header when response takes longer than 200ms
new TimingInterceptor({ thresholdMs: 200 })

// Custom header + threshold
new TimingInterceptor({ headerName: 'X-Request-Duration', thresholdMs: 500 })
```
